domain:
An mRNA vaccine teaches the immune system by providing a genetic blueprint for the production of a specific viral protein, which is then displayed on the surface of cells as an antigen. The mRNA molecule, which is synthesized in a laboratory, contains the genetic instructions for the viral protein, but it does not contain any infectious material from the virus itself.

When the mRNA vaccine is injected into the body, it is taken up by cells, such as immune system cells called antigen-presenting cells (APCs). The APCs read the mRNA instructions and begin to produce the viral protein. The viral protein is then displayed on the surface of the A

neighbor:
The process by which DNA is transcribed into RNA in a cell is called transcription. It is carried out by an enzyme called RNA polymerase, which binds to a specific region of DNA called the promoter.

1. Initiation: RNA polymerase binds to the promoter region of the DNA molecule. This binding causes the DNA double helix to unwind, exposing the template strand for RNA synthesis.

2. Elongation: RNA polymerase moves along the DNA template, synthesizing a single-stranded RNA molecule in the 5' to 3' direction. As it moves, RNA polymerase adds
